query: prognosis prediction in polymyalgia rheumatica at disease onset

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: PMR has an excellent prognosis when the diagnosis is made promptly, and appropriate treatment is initiated. Mortality among individuals with PMR is not significantly increased compared to the general population. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Evaluation -- Polymyalgia Rheumatica and Giant Cell Arteritis
retrieved_docs.content: In a study, among patients with PMR with the persistence of classic symptoms but no cranial GCA-like symptoms, a PET/CT scan was positive for large vessel vasculitis in 60.7%. Inflammatory low back pain, pelvic girdle, and diffuse lower limb pain were also predictors of positive PET/CT scans in these patients. [37] In another study, among patients who required higher doses of steroids or those who had atypical features like low-grade fever and weight loss, among others, 48% had large vessel vasculitis on PET/CT. Elevated CRP values were found to correlate with large vessel vasculitis. [38] In a study where a random sample of 68 patients with "pure" PMR, histological examinations of biopsy specimens of the temporal artery revealed inflammatory changes in only 3 patients (4.4%). [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated ESR is a common feature in PMR. The majority of authors consider ESR >40 mm/h significant. [26] [27] [28] ESR <40 mm/h is present in 5 to 20% of patients. [29] One study revealed ESR values >104 mm/h in 20% of patients. [29] Patients with low ESR usually have a lower frequency of systemic features like fever, weight loss, and anemia. Response to therapy, the frequency of relapses, and the risk of developing GCA among these patients appear to be comparable with high ESR patients. [30] [31] CRP is also typically elevated. CRP was found to be a more sensitive indicator of disease activity in 1 study, while ESR was found to be a superior predictor of relapse. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Evaluation -- Polymyalgia Rheumatica and Giant Cell Arteritis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with PMR should undergo evaluation for features suggestive of GCA at every visit. A routine biopsy of the temporal artery is not recommended. Features like the development of a new headache, vision and jaw symptoms, tenderness and lack of pulses in the temporal artery, lack of pulses in the periphery, the persistence of inflammatory markers, high-grade fever, and refractoriness of classic symptoms are the red flags that should prompt an urgent evaluation for GCA with imaging (chest/abdomen) such as CT angiography (CTA), magnetic resonance angiography (MRA), or PET.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Implementing routine monitoring of inflammatory markers, such as ESR and CRP, in older populations with musculoskeletal complaints can aid in early detection. Moreover, educating the public on lifestyle factors that may impact inflammatory conditions and promoting regular physical activity and a healthy diet may play a role in preventing or mitigating the severity of PMR.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Deterrence and prevention strategies for PMR primarily revolve around early recognition, accurate diagnosis, and timely initiation of appropriate treatment. Since PMR predominantly affects individuals older than 50 years, proactive screening and awareness campaigns among this age group may facilitate early identification of symptoms, enabling prompt medical intervention. Additionally, raising awareness among healthcare professionals about the clinical features and diagnostic criteria for PMR can improve detection rates.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Essential aspects of long-term management include frequent follow-up, vigilance in detecting developing GCA, and managing relapse or refractory disease. Strong coordination and peer-to-peer communication within the interprofessional team are essential for good outcomes and an optimal care model.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: PMR patients have a higher chance of developing inflammatory arthritis. Features of small joint synovitis, younger age, and positive anti-CCP positivity in patients with PMR were associated with the risk of developing inflammatory arthritis. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Evaluation -- Polymyalgia Rheumatica and Giant Cell Arteritis
retrieved_docs.content: PMR and GCA  frequently overlap, and 20% of patients with PMR will get diagnosed with GCA later. In biopsy-proven GCA, PMR features are present in up to 50% of cases. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be thoroughly counseled regarding the risks and benefits of GC treatment. Patients should also be encouraged to adhere to their medication regimens and long-term follow-up to minimize the risk of disease recurrence and potential side effects of medications. Patients should also be made aware of the importance of taking calcium and vitamin D supplements.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Administer 12.5 to 25 mg daily prednisone equivalent as an initial therapy. Taper GCs gradually. Taper to an oral dose of 10 mg daily prednisone equivalent within 4 to 8 weeks. Once remission is achieved, taper daily oral prednisone by 1 mg every 4 weeks until discontinuation Treat for a minimum of 12 months. For relapse, increase oral prednisone to the pre-relapse dose and decrease it gradually (within 4 to 8 weeks) to the dose at which the relapse occurred. Individualize dose-tapering schedules based on regularly monitoring patient disease activity, laboratory markers, and adverse events. Administer lower doses of 7.5 to 10 mg daily for smaller patients with mild symptoms or brittle diabetes. Consider early introduction of methotrexate (MTX) in addition to GCs, particularly in patients at high risk of relapse and/or prolonged therapy, as well as in cases with risk factors, comorbidities, and/or concomitant medications where GC-related adverse events are more likely to occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of PMR is best accomplished using an interprofessional team approach. Patients with PMR usually receive an initial evaluation by primary care clinicians. A low threshold for suspicion, timely referral to rheumatology, and excluding giant cell arteritis are essential aspects in the initial care of these patients. PMR has a long list of differentials and mimickers, and diagnosis may be even more challenging if the patient already has 1 of those as a coexisting condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Relapses often entail increased ESR and CRP and the return of symptoms. Increased risk of relapse is found to correlate with a higher initial dose of steroid used, rapid steroid tapering, HLA-DRB1*0401, and persistently high inflammatory markers. [13] [49] [50] Managing relapses can be challenging. The usual approach is as follows: For patients with recurrent symptoms developing following discontinuation of GCs and accompanied by an elevation in the CRP and/or ESR, GCs can be restarted at the original dose that managed symptoms. For patients who relapse several times, it is reasonable to lengthen the interval between dose reductions to 6 to 8 weeks.

retrieved_docs.title: Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal Arteritis) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of a strong inflammatory response at the time of presentation is a risk factor for a protracted treatment course and a high relapse rate. Symptoms such as fever, weight loss, ESR ≥85 mm/h, and hemoglobin ≤11 g/dL indicate a strong inflammatory response. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Evaluation -- 2012 Provisional Classification Criteria for Polymyalgia Rheumatica: A European League Against Rheumatism/American College of Rheumatology Collaborative Initiative
retrieved_docs.content: "A score=4 had 68% sensitivity and 78% specificity for discriminating all comparison subjects from PMR. The specificity was higher (88%) for discriminating shoulder conditions from PMR and lower (65%) for discriminating RA from PMR. Adding ultrasound, a score=5 had increased sensitivity to 66% and specificity to 81% . These criteria are not meant for diagnostic purposes." [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are also reports of an association between PMR and diverticulitis, which could suggest a role of a change in microbiota and chronic bowel inflammation in the immunopathogenesis of the disease. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1317
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis), الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic cardiac involvement that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when evaluating patients with rheumatic heart disease, as it helps differentiate these acute forms from more chronic rheumatic cardiac conditions and guides timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic endocarditis) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic heart disease that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This abrupt and brief presentation is a key feature across these related conditions, aiding clinicians in recognizing the rapid evolution of rheumatic involvement of the heart’s structures and informing timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1148
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus (الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية) is clinically associated with other circulatory disease, and this category of circulatory disease is classified as a subtype of connective tissue disorder (اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة). This relationship underscores that cardiovascular manifestations observed in lupus are part of the broader spectrum of connective tissue diseases, highlighting the importance of integrated cardiovascular evaluation and management in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 874
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, and acute rheumatic valvulitis are all characterized by a clinical course of sudden onset and/or short duration. This pattern of sudden onset and/or short duration also defines the clinical course of الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic inflammation) and of احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي (acute anterior‑wall myocardial injury). Recognizing this rapid and brief presentation is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of these rheumatic cardiac conditions and related acute cardiac events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, and acute rheumatic valvulitis are each described as having a clinical course characterized by sudden onset and/or short duration. This pattern of abrupt presentation and brief disease course is reflected in the Arabic terminology الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُور الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد, which denotes acute rheumatic inflammation, and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد, which refers to an unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease. Recognizing that these acute rheumatic manifestations share this rapid and limited clinical trajectory aids clinicians in timely diagnosis and appropriate management of rheumatic heart conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1093
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis and acute rheumatic valvulitis are both described as having a clinical course marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. This pattern of abrupt, brief presentation also characterizes the clinical course of الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis) and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic myocarditis). In addition, the same sudden‑onset, short‑duration course applies to احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي (acute formation of the anterior wall of the heart muscle). Together, these relationships indicate that various acute rheumatic cardiac manifestations share a rapid and brief clinical presentation, which is important for prompt recognition and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2049
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, and acute rheumatic valvulitis are all described as having a clinical course that is marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. The same rapid‑onset, brief‑duration pattern is also attributed to التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis) and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (an unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease). Collectively, these forms of acute rheumatic heart disease share the characteristic of a swift and often brief clinical presentation, which is important for timely diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2257
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic valvulitis is characterized by a clinical course of sudden onset and/or short duration. This pattern of sudden onset and/or short duration is also the clinical course of الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis), الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic pericarditis), مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (an acute unspecified rheumatic heart disease), and احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي (acute anterior‑wall myocardial involvement). These rheumatic cardiac conditions therefore share an abrupt and often brief presentation, a feature that is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1056
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis and acute rheumatic valvulitis are both rheumatic heart conditions that typically present with a sudden onset and/or short duration clinical course. The descriptor “Sudden onset AND/OR short duration” is also identified as the clinical course for الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُور الرُّومَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), for مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease), and for احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي (acute anterior‑wall myocardial injury). Thus, a rapid and brief presentation is a common feature linking these various acute rheumatic cardiac manifestations and certain acute myocardial conditions, underscoring the importance of recognizing this pattern for timely diagnosis and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis and acute rheumatic valvulitis are both rheumatic heart conditions whose clinical course is characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. The same rapid and brief clinical pattern is also described for the Arabic‑named entities التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis), الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease). Together, these English and Arabic terms indicate that acute rheumatic involvement of the heart—whether affecting the whole heart (pancarditis), the valves, the myocardium, the pericardium, or an unspecified cardiac component—typically presents with a rapid onset and a relatively short disease course, a feature that is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2836
retrieved_docs.content: Fever of unknown origin is a broader clinical entity that includes fever (الحُمَّى) as a specific manifestation. The condition ssc is linked to both neck pain (ألم في العنق) and fever (الحُمَّى), indicating that patients with ssc may present with cervical discomfort alongside febrile episodes. Clinically, fever (الحُمَّى) is frequently observed in patients with rheumatoid arthritis (التهاب المفاصل الروماتويدي) and gouty arthritis (التهاب المفاصل المعدي), reflecting the inflammatory nature of these arthritides. Additionally, tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with fever (الحُمَّى), suggesting that smoking may predispose individuals to febrile responses or exacerbate underlying infections. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians consider fever as a common sign linking diverse rheumatologic disorders, specific symptom clusters, and lifestyle risk factors, thereby guiding comprehensive assessment and management.
